# 2015 Flare-On Challenge 6

*All the Flare-On annual challenges can be found [here](https://flare-on.com/).*

## Executive Summary

This write-up covers the sixth challenge of the 2015 Flare-On series. The objective is to recover a hidden flag embedded inside an Android application package. The solution involves extracting the APK contents, locating and analyzing a native ARM shared library in Ghidra, reversing a prime factorization-based validation scheme applied to pairs of input characters, extracting 23 reference exponent tables from the binary, and inverting the encoding in Python to reconstruct the original flag.

| Tool | Purpose |
|---|---|
| apktool | APK extraction and resource unpacking |
| Ghidra | Static disassembly and decompilation of the ARM `.so` library |
| Python | Reversal script for reconstructing the flag from factorization tables |

---

## 1. Initial Triage

The challenge ships with a single file: `android.apk`. The `.apk` extension indicates an Android application package, which is a ZIP archive containing compiled Dalvik bytecode, resources, and optionally native libraries.

Extracting the contents using `apktool` produces the standard APK directory structure. Inspecting the extracted tree, the `lib/` folder contains a compiled native shared object file with the `.so` extension.

Native libraries in Android APKs are compiled ELF shared objects targeting the device's CPU architecture. This one targets ARM, which requires a disassembler that handles ARM calling conventions and instruction sets. The library is loaded into Ghidra for further examination.

## 2. Static Analysis

### 2.1 Identifying the Entry Point

Browsing Ghidra's symbol tree for JNI exports surfaces one immediately relevant function:

```c
void Java_com_flareon_flare_ValidateActivity_validate(
    int *param_1, undefined4 param_2, undefined4 param_3)
```

The naming convention `Java_<package>_<class>_<method>` identifies this as a JNI-exported function. It is called by the Android activity when the user submits input, making it the validator that determines whether a given string is the correct flag.

### 2.2 Understanding the Validator Structure

After renaming variables to reflect their purpose, the decompiled function reads as follows:

```c
void Java_com_flareon_flare_ValidateActivity_validate
            (int *param_1, undefined4 param_2, undefined4 param_3)
{
  ushort uVar1;
  char *result;
  size_t lengthResult;
  uint extraout_r1;
  code *callBeforeShowResult;
  uint encodedByte;
  uint byteConcat;
  int currentStep;
  uint i;
  int mainCondition;
  undefined4 auStack_1b9c [23];
  short local_1b40 [3478];
  bool isCorrect;
  byte letter;

  memset(local_1b40, 0, 0x1b28);
  memcpy(auStack_1b9c, &PTR_DAT_00015004, 0x5c);

  result = (char *)(**(code **)(*param_1 + 0x2a4))(param_1, param_3, 0);
  if ((result == (char *)0x0) || (lengthResult = strlen(result), 0x2e < lengthResult)) {
    (**(code **)(*param_1 + 0x2a8))(param_1, param_3, result);
    callBeforeShowResult = *(code **)(*param_1 + 0x29c);
    result = "No";
  }
  else {
    mainCondition = 0;
    isCorrect = true;

    for (i = 0; lengthResult = strlen(result), i < lengthResult; i = i + 2) {
      memset(local_1b40, 0, 0x1b28);
      letter = result[i];
      encodedByte = 0;
      if ((letter != 0) && (encodedByte = (uint)letter, result[i + 1] != '\0')) {
        byteConcat = (uint)CONCAT11(letter, result[i + 1]);
        encodedByte = byteConcat & -(uint)(byteConcat < 0x7e7f);
      }
      currentStep = 0;

      do {
        uVar1 = *(ushort *)("\x02" + currentStep);
        while (__aeabi_uidivmod(encodedByte, (uint)uVar1), (extraout_r1 & 0xffff) == 0) {
          *(short *)((int)local_1b40 + currentStep) =
              *(short *)((int)local_1b40 + currentStep) + 1;
          byteConcat = __udivsi3(encodedByte, (uint)uVar1);
          encodedByte = byteConcat & 0xffff;
          if (encodedByte < 2) goto LAB_00010f14;
        }
        currentStep = currentStep + 2;
      } while (currentStep != 0x1b28);

LAB_00010f14:
      currentStep = memcmp((void *)auStack_1b9c[mainCondition], local_1b40, 0xd94);
      if (currentStep == 0) {
        mainCondition = mainCondition + 1;
      }
      else {
        isCorrect = false;
      }
    }

    callBeforeShowResult = *(code **)(*param_1 + 0x29c);
    if ((mainCondition == 0x17) && (isCorrect)) {
      result = "That\'s it!";
    }
    else {
      result = "No";
    }
  }

  (*callBeforeShowResult)(param_1, result);
  return;
}
```

### 2.3 Reversing the Encoding Scheme

Three distinct stages compose the validation logic.

**Stage 1 - Pair encoding.** The input string is consumed two characters at a time. Each pair is combined into a 16-bit value:

```
encodedByte = (char[i] << 8) | char[i+1]
if encodedByte >= 0x7e7f: encodedByte = 0
```

`CONCAT11` is a Ghidra intrinsic for `(uint16_t)(a << 8) | b`. The branchless mask `& -(uint)(byteConcat < 0x7e7f)` zeroes the value if it meets or exceeds the threshold, restricting valid inputs to the printable ASCII range.

**Stage 2 - Prime factorization.** The 16-bit value is factorized into its prime components. Each prime's exponent is accumulated into a slot of the `local_1b40` array, which serves as a sparse exponent vector indexed by prime position:

```
for each prime p at index idx:
    while encodedByte % p == 0:
        local_1b40[idx] += 1
        encodedByte /= p
    if encodedByte < 2: break
```

The ARM helper `__aeabi_uidivmod` computes both quotient and remainder simultaneously; the remainder landing in `extraout_r1` drives the divisibility check.

**Stage 3 - Comparison against reference tables.** The populated exponent vector is compared against one of 23 pre-computed reference tables stored in the binary at `PTR_DAT_00015004` and copied into `auStack_1b9c` at startup. A match advances `mainCondition`; a mismatch sets `isCorrect = false`. The flag is accepted only when all 23 pairs pass, satisfying `mainCondition == 0x17`.

The input length constraint `0x2e < lengthResult` confirms the flag is at most 46 characters: exactly 23 pairs of two bytes each.

### 2.4 Extracting the Reference Tables

Each reference table is a flat array of `uint16_t` values representing the prime exponent vector for a particular character pair. The tables are large but sparse, with most entries being zero, because typical ASCII values have few and small prime factors. All 23 tables are dumped from the binary by navigating to `PTR_DAT_00015004` in Ghidra's memory view and extracting each blob in sequence for use in the reversal script.

## 3. Extracting the Flag

With the encoding pipeline fully understood, the original flag can be recovered by brute-forcing each 16-bit value in `[1, 0x7e7f)`, computing its prime factorization exponent vector, and matching it against the corresponding reference table. The two bytes recovered from each match are appended to reconstruct the flag in order.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import struct

raw_tables = [
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [0] @ 0x0003a5d0
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [1] @ 0x00038aa8
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [2] @ 0x00036f80
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [3] @ 0x00035458
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [4] @ 0x00033930
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [5] @ 0x00031e08
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [6] @ 0x000302e0
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [7] @ 0x0002e7b8
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [8] @ 0x0002cc90
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [9] @ 0x0002b168
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [10] @ 0x00029640
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [11] @ 0x00027b18
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [12] @ 0x00025ff0
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [13] @ 0x000244c8
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [14] @ 0x000229a0
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [15] @ 0x00020e78
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [16] @ 0x0001f350
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [17] @ 0x0001d828
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [18] @ 0x0001bd00
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [19] @ 0x0001a1d8
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [20] @ 0x000186b0
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [21] @ 0x00016b88
    bytes.fromhex(""),  # [22] @ 0x00015060
]

def build_primes(limit=0x7e7f):
    sieve = bytearray([1]) * (limit + 1)
    for i in range(2, int(limit**0.5) + 1):
        if sieve[i]:
            for j in range(i*i, limit + 1, i):
                sieve[j] = 0
    return [i for i in range(2, limit + 1) if sieve[i]]

primes = build_primes()
flag = ""

for i, raw in enumerate(raw_tables):
    target = list(struct.unpack_from(f"<{len(raw)//2}H", raw))

    for val in range(1, 0x7e7f):
        n = val
        counts = [0] * (len(raw) // 2)
        for idx, p in enumerate(primes):
            if idx >= len(counts): break
            while n % p == 0:
                counts[idx] += 1
                n //= p
            if n < 2: break
        if counts == target:
            flag += chr(val >> 8) + chr(val & 0xFF)
            print(f"[{i}] -> '{chr(val>>8)}{chr(val&0xFF)}'")
            break

print(f"\nFlag: {flag}")
```

The script unpacks each reference blob as a sequence of little-endian `uint16_t` values to reconstruct the expected exponent vector, then iterates over all candidate values, factorizes each one using the same prime table the native code uses, and stops when the exponent counts match. Splitting the matched value into its high and low bytes recovers the original character pair.

**Flag: `Should_havg0ne_to_tashi_$tation@flare-on.com`**
